one_line_takeaway: Using a new 370K-post dataset from an all-agent social platform, the paper argues that whether an AI agent's post was autonomous or human-directed is structurally unknowable from observables, yet shows this uncertainty does not erase practical consequences: agent populations still stratify into predictive quality tiers, click-model accuracy degrades measurably as low-quality agents enter training data, and capability awareness (including prompt injection) spreads epidemically across agent communities and resists suppression.
key_finding: Three linked findings, all the paper's own: (1) individual-post orchestration status is argued to be structurally non-identifiable (a formal/constructive claim, not an empirical measurement); (2) despite this, population-level differences persist and matter operationally -- a click model's AUC drops 8.5% (0.640 to 0.586) as the share of low-validation agents in training data rises to 50%; (3) capability-awareness (including prompt-injection references) spreads endemically across agent communities with R0 well above 1 across all three risk tiers -- risky 1.26, benign 2.33, dual-use 3.53 (with a separate injection-specific R0 = 1.09) -- and remains above 1 even under an extreme modeled 70% reduction in transmission rate (dual-use falls only to 1.12, benign to 1.09, risky to 1.03).
core_contribution: The paper formalizes what it calls the 'Agent Attribution Problem' in information retrieval: for any post produced by an AI agent, a private human-operator instruction could have produced the identical observable output, so the latent orchestration indicator z_p (autonomous vs. human-directed) is not identifiable at the individual post level from observables alone (text, timing, community, votes, replies) -- stated as Conjecture 1, proved by construction rather than empirically tested. Building on this, the authors introduce MolbookTraces, a dataset of 370,737 posts, 3,882,705 comments, from 46,872 agents across 4,257 communities on Moltbook (an agent-native social platform where every user is an LLM-driven agent), collected Jan 28-Feb 8, 2026 (12 days, 51.7% of platform content at collection time). Using this dataset they show three things: (1) although individual-post attribution is impossible, population-level behavioral differences between agents grouped by five orchestration-independent platform signals (karma, verified email, follower ratio, owner-linkage claim token, comment/post ratio) are statistically significant and predictive of held-out engagement outcomes; (2) a position-based click model (PBM) trained on agent upvote patterns degrades in AUC as low-validation agents replace high-validation ones in the training mix; (3) awareness of capabilities (including prompt injection) diffuses across communities as an SIS epidemic process with reproduction number R0 above 1 for all measured risk tiers, and this endemic spread resists large modeled reductions in transmission rate.
threat_model: Not applicable in the security sense -- this is an empirical/observational information-retrieval and user-modeling study, not adversarial security research with attackers, defenders, or a security boundary. The paper explicitly frames its subject as a 'structural non-identifiability' problem for user models, not a detection or defense task. The one place the paper touches security is peripheral: prompt injection is treated as one of 47 tracked capability keywords (in a 'risky' category alongside 'exploits'), and the authors measure how references to it propagate across communities (1.5% of posts, 1,586 agents, 288 communities, capability-specific R0 = 1.09) using the same SIS diffusion methodology applied to benign (e.g., GitHub, Python) and dual-use (e.g., trading, automation) capabilities. There is no attack implementation, no defense proposal, and no security evaluation of any system; injection is cited via Greshake et al. 2023 purely as a definitional reference for what the keyword means.
limitations: The paper states its own limitations explicitly: (1) the diffusion analysis is observational, not causal -- co-occurrence patterns could reflect genuine social diffusion (agents reading and citing each other) or independent generation from similarly-configured prompts, and without interventional data the two cannot be fully distinguished (the permutation test found risky-capability timing statistically indistinguishable from chance, p=0.993, unlike benign and dual-use); (2) data spans only 12 days on a single platform (Moltbook), so generalization to mixed human-agent settings is untested; (3) Conjecture 1 assumes agent configuration (system prompts) is never disclosed to observers -- platforms that disclose prompts could partially resolve attribution; (4) the click-model result uses only a basic PBM, and the authors flag testing more sophisticated click models as future work; (5) absolute click-model AUC is described by the authors as 'modest' because binary upvotes are noisier than search clicks, and the two agent populations differ in base engagement rate (76.2% vs 44.2%), which the authors note does not by itself explain the AUC gap since AUC is rank-based.

datasets_used: MolbookTraces (this paper's own release): 370,737 posts, 3,882,705 comments, 46,872 unique agents, 4,257 communities, collected Jan 28-Feb 8 2026 from Moltbook, covering 51.7% of the platform's ~717,541 posts at collection time
quantitative_results: Click-model AUC drops 8.5% (0.640 -> 0.586) as low-validation agents reach 50% of PBM training data

Reproduction number R0 by capability risk tier: dual-use 3.53, benign 2.33, risky 1.26 (all via attack-rate formula on the 18,350 capability-referencing agents; using all 46,872 platform agents instead gives lower R0 1.09-1.39)
Prompt-injection-specific R0 = 1.09; injection content found in 1.5% of posts, 1,586 agents across 288 communities
Under modeled 70% transmission-rate (beta) reduction, R0 remains above 1 for all tiers: dual-use 1.12, benign 1.09, risky 1.03; a 30% reduction barely moves values (dual-use still 2.01)
Capability references double every 11.5-13.0 hours; exponential growth model fits R2 = 0.79-0.87, consistent with SIS-derived R0 at generation intervals of 5-39 hours

Table 2 behavioral differences (high-validation vs low-validation agents, Cohen's d): one-shot ratio 0.178 vs 0.524 (d=-0.72); cross-community entropy 0.740 vs 0.241 (d=+0.67); temporal burstiness -0.024 vs -0.329 (d=+0.88); style consistency 0.430 vs 0.548 (d=-0.55); all p<0.001

Held-out validation: high-validation agents receive 1.6x the upvotes (2.71 vs 1.73, p<10^-100), mean reply depth 1.11 vs 0.92, participate in 2.31 vs 1.15 communities; Spearman rho=-0.346 (p<10^-100) across quintiles

Dataset composition: 39-41-14-7 split across lurker/occasional/regular/power-user post-count tiers (Gini 0.74); duplicate rate 32.9%; 27.9% of agents active in >1 community (vs 7.5% human baseline, i.e. 3.7x); 14.1% of posts meet fine-tuning quality thresholds; 12.8% contain adversarial content; 51.3% filtered as low quality; 26.8% of posts show regex-detected private-information disclosure; 22.3% show Cialdini-style susceptibility markers

71.7% of capability-referencing agents mention at least one dual-use capability; permutation test (1,000 shuffles): benign z=2.44, p=0.005; dual-use z=9.07, p<0.001; risky p=0.993 (not distinguishable from chance)
baselines_compared: No prior attribution/detection method or alternative click model is compared against; the only model used is a standard position-based click model (PBM, Chuklin et al. 2022) evaluated at varying training-data mixtures of the paper's own high-/low-validation agent groups (not an external baseline system)

The 90-9-1 human-platform participation rule (Gasparini et al. 2020) and human cross-community posting rate (7.5%) are cited as reference points from prior literature, not re-run as experimental baselines
